EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifted northeast through the Western Atlantic and passed offshore of the Southeastern New England Shoreline. Snow accumulations on the order of 3 to 8 inches, with lighter amounts out on the Cape and Islands, were attributable with this storm. The light and fluffy nature of the snow combined with strong winds associated with the storm resulted in blowing and drifing, as well as visibility restrictions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 to 7.5 inches of snow fell across Northwest Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving across the region produced showers and thunderstorms. With a cold pool aloft (colder than normal temperatures at 500mb), these thunderstorms produced penny to nickel size hail.

A series of low pressure centers impacted the Southern New England Region with a combination of heavy snows and ice. The first area of low pressure on February 1st lifted northeastward offshore of the Southeastern New England shoreline ushering heavy snows across the interior portions of New England, especially north and west. A second area of low pressure deepened through the Ohio River Valley, redeveloping over the Southeastern New England shoreline bringing a combination of heavy snows, sleet and freezing rain over much of the region February 2nd, before changing back to all snow into the end of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 6 inches of snow fell across Southeast Providence County over the two day period, with upwards of a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation for isolated locations falling during the morning period on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge brought very hot temperatures to Southern New England. A moist southwest low level flow increased humidity levels such that heat index values rose above 105 degrees for a period of a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Newport State Airport (KUUU) recorded heat indexes of 106 to 110 over a five hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge brought very hot temperatures to Southern New England. A moist southwest low level flow increased humidity levels such that heat index values rose above 105 degrees for a period of a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Weather Observation System at North Central State Airport (KSFZ) near Smithfield recorded heat indexes of 105 to 107 over a five hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure centers impacted the Southern New England Region with a combination of heavy snows and ice. The first area of low pressure on February 1st lifted northeastward offshore of the Southeastern New England shoreline ushering heavy snows across the interior portions of New England, especially north and west. A second area of low pressure deepened through the Ohio River Valley, redeveloping over the Southeastern New England shoreline bringing a combination of heavy snows, sleet and freezing rain over much of the region February 2nd, before changing back to all snow into the end of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 6 inches of snow fell across Eastern Kent County over the two day period, with upwards of a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation for isolated locations falling during the morning period on the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure deepened through the interior Mid-Atlantic Region early on February 25th slowly moving to the northeast and reinvigorating off the immediate shoreline of New England and into the Gulf of Maine before retreating further northeast into Nova Scotia. Heavy snows impacted interior New England during the onset of the storm, while high winds developed across the eastern shoreline as the storm deepened as the storm continued to move northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Station at Westerly State Airport (KWST) recorded a sustained windspeed of 35 mph with gusts to 51 mph.  In addition, multiple trees were downed on Shumankanuc Hill Road in Charlestown and trees were downed in Narragansett on Route 1A near the intersection with Route 138 and at the intersection of Robinson and Rodman Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare and historic October Nor'easter brought very heavy snow to portions of southern New England on Saturday October 29.  Low pressure tracked northeast from the North Carolina coast Saturday morning, rapidly strengthening as it passed well south of Nantucket Saturday evening. As the storm intensified, colder air from aloft was drawn into New England resulting in heavy snow in the interior. ||Snowfall accumulations of one to two feet were common in the Monadnocks, Berkshires, Connecticut Valley, and higher elevations in central Massachusetts. Up to 31 inches of snow was reported at Jaffrey, New Hampshire and Plainfield, Massachusetts. Minor accumulations were even reported down to the south coast as the rain changed to a period of snow late Saturday night before ending. The accumulation of the heavy wet snow on trees and power lines resulted in widespread tree damage and power outages across many communities in central and western Massachusetts, southern New Hampshire, and northeastern Connecticut. ||The precipitation started as mainly snow early Saturday afternoon across the interior of southern New England, although a brief period of rain at the onset was common across the lower elevations. Several hours of heavy snow occurred from mid afternoon through late evening on Saturday October 29. Snowfall rates reached 3 inches per hour for several hours in the Berkshires and Monadnocks as a nearly stationary band of heavy snow set up across this region. The snow tapered off just after midnight Saturday night in western New England with the last of the precipitation exiting eastern New England Sunday morning. This storm also brought damaging winds to Cape Cod and the islands with wind gusts up to 70 mph occurring early Sunday morning October 30 as well as minor to moderate coastal flooding to east coastal Massachusetts during the high tide early Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of snow fell across eastern Kent County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the mid-Atlantic states moved up the coast passing southeast of Southern New England.  This produced heavy rainfall and strong to damaging winds across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 63 mph wind gust was recorded on a citizen's home weather station in Burrillville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through Southern New England resulting in increased moisture and lift.  This produced thunderstorms across the area, including northern Connecticut, Rhode Island, and the coastal waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing Station at T.F. Green Airport in Warwick, RI (KPVD) recorded sustained wind speeds of 43 mph and gusts to 60 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the east coast bringing heavy rain and strong to damaging winds to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer recorded a 54 mph wind gust on their home weather station in North Foster.  Numerous small trees were downed throughout Scituate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the coast and southeast of Nantucket producing up to a foot of snow across Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine to eleven inches of snow fell across Washington County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to seven inches of snow fell across Newport County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve to seventeen inches of snow fell across northwest Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the mid-Atlantic states moved up the coast passing southeast of Southern New England.  This produced heavy rainfall and strong to damaging winds across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weatherflow reporting site at Point Judith recorded a wind gust of 79 mph.  A mesonet site in Charlestown recorded a wind gust to 68 mph.  Wires were downed at the intersection of Ava and Ella Streets in Westerly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the east coast bringing heavy rain and strong to damaging winds to Southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator recorded a wind gust of 64 mph on their home weather station in Barrington.  In Warren, large limbs and wires were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved up the coast and southeast of Nantucket producing up to a foot of snow across Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine to eleven inches of snow fell across southeast Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing nor'easter coastal storm dumped nearly two feet of snow across portions of Rhode Island in a 24 hour period.||This was the second major storm of an above average winter of snowfall. The first occurred December 26 and 27, with several other relatively minor snowfalls in the month of January, and a third major storm February 1 and 2. With only a brief thaw in between the December storm and the January storm, snow piled up across southern New England resulting in numerous roof collapses, towns seeking permission to dump excess snow in area rivers and bays, and numerous disruptions to transportation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten to twenty-two inches of snow fell across northwest Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Area of low pressure developed and moved up the eastern shoreline bringing a mix of snow and ice for the New England region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2 to 4 inches of snow fell across Northwest Providence County followed by a third of an inch of ice accumulation over isolated locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved across Southern New England and stalled just south of the area.  This front was instrumental in bringing tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee into New England.  A series of shortwaves moved through the northeast during this time period bringing several periods of showers and steady rainfall to parts of Southern New England.  Rainfall totals throughout the region over the four days totaled anywhere from two to eight inches, with most areas receiving four to six inches.  This resulted in flooding both on the rivers and small streams and in urban areas.  The bulk of the flooding in urban areas occurred on Sept. 8 as a band of very heavy rain moved through, dumping up to two inches of rain in an hour to hour and a half in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded, including Route 117, which had a foot of water over it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved across Southern New England and stalled just south of the area.  This front was instrumental in bringing tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee into New England.  A series of shortwaves moved through the northeast during this time period bringing several periods of showers and steady rainfall to parts of Southern New England.  Rainfall totals throughout the region over the four days totaled anywhere from two to eight inches, with most areas receiving four to six inches.  This resulted in flooding both on the rivers and small streams and in urban areas.  The bulk of the flooding in urban areas occurred on Sept. 8 as a band of very heavy rain moved through, dumping up to two inches of rain in an hour to hour and a half in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cars were stranded on Oaklawn and Wilbur Avenues in Cranston with water nearly to the cars roofs.  In Providence, the northbound portion of Route 10 from Union Avenue to Westminster Street was closed due to flooding from 0903am EST to 0924am EST.  The southbound portion of Route 10 at Reservoir Avenue were closed as well and reopened at 1030am EST.  Numerous cars were stranded in floodwaters in Cranston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved across Southern New England and stalled just south of the area.  This front was instrumental in bringing tropical moisture from the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee into New England.  A series of shortwaves moved through the northeast during this time period bringing several periods of showers and steady rainfall to parts of Southern New England.  Rainfall totals throughout the region over the four days totaled anywhere from two to eight inches, with most areas receiving four to six inches.  This resulted in flooding both on the rivers and small streams and in urban areas.  The bulk of the flooding in urban areas occurred on Sept. 8 as a band of very heavy rain moved through, dumping up to two inches of rain in an hour to hour and a half in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets throughout Woonsocket were closed due to flooding.  This included northbound and southbound lanes of Route 146.  All lanes reopened by noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance rotating through a strong cyclonic low produced showers and thunderstorms across Southern New England.  One of these storms became severe producing wind damage in northeast Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires on Wrentham Road were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hurricane Irene formed east of the Caribbean island of Dominica, part of the Lesser Antilles region, on the afternoon of August 20. Irene moved through the Caribbean and up the east coast of the United States making landfall twice. She first made landfall as a Category 1 Hurricane near Cape Lookout, North Carolina around 7:30am on August 27, then moved offshore again during the evening. She then made a 2nd landfall, again as a Category 1 Hurricane at 540am on August 28 near Little Egg Inlet in New Jersey. She moved over New York City and then into southeastern New York State and Connecticut as a Tropical Storm a few hours later. By the end of the evening of the 28th, Irene was crossing the U.S./Canada border having produced significant amounts of rain, storm surge, inland and coastal flooding, and wind damage across southern New England and much of the east coast of the United States. ||In Southern New England, the minimum surface pressure recorded was 976.9mb taken at Barnes Municipal Airport in Westfield, Massachusetts. The storm surge experienced along the coast was generally in the two to four foot range with a high of 4.78 feet at Fox Point in Providence, Rhode Island. The highest sustained windspeed was 54 knots (62 mph) at the Physical Oceanographic Real Time System station at Conimicut Light in Narragansett Bay, RI. The highest sustained wind speed on land was 38 knots (44 mph) recorded on the Automated Surface Observing Systems at both Barnstable Municipal Airport in Hyannis, MA (KHYA) and Logan International Airport in Boston, MA (KBOS). Rainfall amounts ranged from nearly zero (0.03 at Nantucket Memorial Airport - ACK) to nearly 10 inches (9.92 in Conway, MA). ||Despite the relatively low wind speeds, sustained winds over a 6 to 12 hour long duration resulted in widespread tree damage and resulted in power outages to roughly half a million customers throughout the state. Some of these customers did not get their power back until the Friday following the storm (some five days later). Durring the passage of Tropical Storm Irene, the winds resulted in $0.19M in property damages. ||The collective effects of Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, resulted in 1 fatality, 0 injuries, and $127.3M in property damage in the following counties: Barnstable, Bristol, Essex, Franklin, Hampden, Hampshire, Middlesex, Nantucket, Norfolk, Plymouth, Suffolk, and Worcester (all in MA), Hartford, Tolland, and Windham (all in CT), Cheshire and Hillsborough (all in NH), and Bristol, Providence, Kent, Washington, and Newport (all in RI).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees in Warwick were downed, including on Chester Avenue and Post Road.  The Automated Surface Observing System at T.F. Green State Airport (KPVD) recorded sustained wind speeds of 38 knots (44 mph) and gusts to 56 knots (64 mph).

One of these trees was downed onto a car.

A trained spotter recorded gusts to 51 knots (59 mph) on their home weather station in Newport.  An amateur radio operator recorded gusts to 49 knots (57 mph) on their home weather station in Little Compton.  The Automated Surface Observing Station at Newport State Airport (KUUU) recorded sustained winds of 30 knots (35 mph) and wind gusts to 48 knots (55 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Mesoscale Convective System moved out of the Great Lakes and across New York state providing a focus for convection across southern New England.  One overnight thunderstorm produced a severe microburst in Providence, RI that downed numerous trees throughout town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 4 to 6 inch diameter trees were downed in the Veterans Memorial Parkway area.  Also, a utility pole was downed across a road in the same area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees, utility poles, utility lines, and wires were downed across North Providence and Providence as the result of a microburst.  This included 42 trees in Roger Williams Park and more than 200 trees on city property.  Trees were downed onto cars and buildings.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree on Highland Street was downed by thunderstorm winds.

A Mesoscale Convective System moved out of the Great Lakes and across New York state providing a focus for convection across southern New England.  One overnight thunderstorm produced a severe microburst in Providence, RI that downed numerous trees throughout town.

A strong upper level ridge brought very hot temperatures to Southern New England. A moist southwest low level flow increased humidity levels such that heat index values rose above 105 degrees for a period of a few hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at T.F. Green State Airport (KPVD) recorded heat indexes of 105 to 106 over an eight hour period.
